av.title: Algorithm Visualizer
av.complexity.title: Complexity & Properties
bubble-sort.name: Bubble Sort
bubble-sort.modes.standard.label: Standard
bubble-sort.modes.standard.desc: Classic Bubble Sort: repeatedly steps through the array, compares adjacent elements, and swaps them if they are in the wrong order. Each pass bubbles the largest unsorted element to its correct position.
bubble-sort.details.standard.principles: Compare adjacent elements pairwise from left to right
Swap if the left element is greater than the right element
After each pass, the largest unsorted element "bubbles up" to its final position
Optimization: if no swaps occur during a pass, the array is already sorted — stop early
Each pass reduces the unsorted portion by one element

bubble-sort.details.standard.concepts.definition: One complete traversal of the unsorted portion of the array. After pass k, the k-th largest element is in its correct position.
The fundamental operation: compare arr[j] with arr[j+1] and swap if arr[j] > arr[j+1]. This moves larger values toward the end.
If a complete pass produces no swaps, the array is sorted. This optimization gives O(n) best-case time for already-sorted input.
Bubble Sort never swaps equal elements (only swaps when strictly greater), preserving the relative order of equal values.

bubble-sort.details.standard.tradeoffs.p: Extremely simple to understand and implement
Stable sorting algorithm — preserves order of equal elements
In-place — requires only O(1) extra memory
Adaptive — performs well on nearly sorted data with early termination
Good for educational purposes and small datasets
bubble-sort.details.standard.tradeoffs.c: O(n²) average and worst case — very slow for large arrays
Significantly slower than O(n log n) algorithms like Merge Sort or Quick Sort
Many unnecessary comparisons even when elements are far from their target position
Poor cache performance due to sequential access pattern with many swaps
bubble-sort.details.standard.tradeoffs.w: Best for educational purposes, very small arrays (< 50 elements), or nearly sorted data where early termination provides a real benefit. For production use with large datasets, prefer Quick Sort, Merge Sort, or Tim Sort.

bubble-sort.depRules.name: Stable
bubble-sort.depRules.role: Yes — equal elements maintain relative order

bubble-sort.depRules.name: Adaptive
bubble-sort.depRules.role: Yes — faster on nearly sorted data

selection-sort.name: Selection Sort
selection-sort.modes.standard.label: Standard
selection-sort.modes.standard.desc: Classic Selection Sort: finds the minimum element in the unsorted portion and swaps it with the first unsorted element. Each pass places one element in its final position with at most one swap.
selection-sort.details.standard.principl: Divide the array into sorted (left) and unsorted (right) portions
Find the minimum element in the unsorted portion by scanning left to right
Swap the minimum with the first unsorted element
Move the sorted boundary one position to the right
Repeat until the entire array is sorted

selection-sort.details.standard.concepts.definition: The core operation: scan the unsorted portion to find the minimum element. This requires n−i−1 comparisons in pass i.
Selection Sort uses only O(1) extra memory — a single variable to track the minimum index. No auxiliary arrays needed.
Swapping the minimum to the front can jump over equal elements, changing their relative order. For example, [2a, 2b, 1] becomes [1, 2b, 2a].
Each pass performs at most one swap, giving exactly n−1 swaps in the worst case. This is optimal and far fewer than Bubble Sort.

selection-sort.details.standard.tradeoff: Minimizes the number of swaps — O(n) swaps vs O(n²) for Bubble Sort
In-place — requires only O(1) extra memory
Performance is predictable — always O(n²) regardless of input order
Good when writes/swaps are expensive (e.g., flash memory)
O(n²) in all cases — no best-case improvement for sorted input
Not stable — equal elements may change relative order
Not adaptive — does not benefit from partially sorted data
Significantly slower than O(n log n) algorithms for large datasets

Best when swap cost is high and comparison cost is low, for small datasets, or when simplicity is valued over performance. When stability matters, prefer Insertion Sort. For large datasets, prefer Quick Sort, Merge Sort, or Tim Sort.

selection-sort.depRules.name: Time (Best)
selection-sort.depRules.role: O(n²) — always scans entire unsorted portion

selection-sort.depRules.name: Stable
selection-sort.depRules.role: No — swaps can change relative order of equal elements

selection-sort.depRules.name: Swaps
selection-sort.depRules.role: O(n) — at most n−1 swaps (fewer than Bubble Sort)

insertion-sort.name: Insertion Sort
insertion-sort.modes.standard.label: Standard
insertion-sort.modes.standard.desc: Classic Insertion Sort: builds a sorted portion from left to right by taking each element and inserting it into its correct position among the already-sorted elements by shifting larger elements one position to the right.
insertion-sort.details.standard.principl: Start with the first element as a sorted portion of size one
Take the next unsorted element (key) and compare it leftward with sorted elements
Shift each sorted element that is greater than the key one position to the right
Insert the key into the vacated position
Repeat until all elements are in the sorted portion

insertion-sort.details.standard.concepts.definition: The current element being inserted into the sorted portion. In pass i, the key is arr[i], compared against arr[i−1], arr[i−2], … until its correct position is found.
Moving sorted elements one position to the right to make room for the key. Equivalent to a series of adjacent swaps moving the key leftward.
Insertion Sort only swaps when strictly greater (not equal), so equal elements preserve their original relative order.
On nearly sorted input, the inner loop terminates early for most elements, giving O(n) best-case time. The fewer inversions, the faster it runs.

insertion-sort.details.standard.tradeoff: Stable sorting algorithm — preserves order of equal elements
In-place — requires only O(1) extra memory
Adaptive — very efficient on nearly sorted or small datasets
Online — can sort elements as they are received
O(n²) average and worst case — slow for large arrays
Significantly slower than O(n log n) algorithms like Merge Sort or Quick Sort
Many shifts required when elements are far from their target position
Performance degrades significantly on reverse-sorted data

Best for small arrays (< 50 elements), nearly sorted data, or as the base case in hybrid algorithms like Tim Sort. Preferred over Selection Sort when stability is required. For large datasets, prefer Quick Sort, Merge Sort, or Tim Sort.

insertion-sort.depRules.name: Stable
insertion-sort.depRules.role: Yes — equal elements maintain relative order

insertion-sort.depRules.name: Adaptive
insertion-sort.depRules.role: Yes — faster on nearly sorted data

merge-sort.name: Merge Sort
merge-sort.modes.standard.label: Standard
merge-sort.modes.standard.desc: Classic top-down Merge Sort: recursively divides the array in half until sub-arrays have one element, then merges pairs of sorted sub-arrays back together by comparing and placing elements in order. Guarantees O(n log n) time in all cases.
merge-sort.details.standard.principles: Recursively divide the array into two halves until each sub-array has one element
A single-element sub-array is inherently sorted (base case)
Merge two sorted sub-arrays by comparing their front elements and placing the smaller one first
Continue merging until all elements are combined into one fully sorted array
The divide step takes O(log n) levels; each level does O(n) work during merges

merge-sort.details.standard.concepts.definition: Split the array at the midpoint into left and right halves. This continues recursively until sub-arrays have one element each.
Combine two sorted sub-arrays into one sorted array by repeatedly picking the smaller front element. Requires O(n) auxiliary space for temporary storage.
When left and right elements are equal, the left element is placed first, preserving the original relative order of equal values.
Unlike Quick Sort, Merge Sort always divides evenly and always does O(n) work per level. There is no worst-case degradation.

merge-sort.details.standard.tradeoffs.pr: Guaranteed O(n log n) time in all cases — no worst-case degradation
Stable sorting algorithm — preserves order of equal elements
Excellent for sorting linked lists — no random access needed
Predictable performance — not sensitive to input order
Natural choice for external sorting (sorting data too large for memory)
merge-sort.details.standard.tradeoffs.co: Requires O(n) auxiliary space for merging — not in-place
Slower than Quick Sort in practice due to higher constant factors and cache misses
Overhead of recursive function calls for small sub-arrays
Memory allocation for temporary arrays can be expensive
merge-sort.details.standard.tradeoffs.wh: Use Merge Sort when stability is required, when guaranteed O(n log n) worst-case is needed, for linked lists, or for external sorting. For in-memory arrays where space is a concern, prefer Quick Sort.

merge-sort.depRules.name: Time (Best)
merge-sort.depRules.role: O(n log n) — always divides and merges

merge-sort.depRules.name: Stable
merge-sort.depRules.role: Yes — equal elements maintain relative order

merge-sort.depRules.name: Adaptive
merge-sort.depRules.role: No — always O(n log n) regardless of input

quick-sort.name: Quick Sort
quick-sort.modes.standard.label: Standard
quick-sort.modes.standard.desc: Classic Quick Sort with Lomuto partition: selects the last element as pivot, partitions the array so that elements smaller than pivot are on the left and larger on the right, then recursively sorts both sides.
quick-sort.details.standard.principles: Choose a pivot element (here: last element in the sub-array)
Partition: rearrange so all elements ≤ pivot are on the left, all > pivot are on the right
Place the pivot in its final sorted position between the two groups
Recursively apply the same process to the left and right sub-arrays
Base case: sub-arrays of size 0 or 1 are already sorted

quick-sort.details.standard.concepts.definition: The element chosen to divide the array. After partitioning, the pivot is in its final sorted position. Lomuto scheme uses the last element.
Scans left to right maintaining a boundary index i. When an element ≤ pivot is found, it is swapped to position i and i advances. After the scan, the pivot is swapped to position i.
Quick Sort partitions within the original array using only O(log n) stack space for recursion, unlike Merge Sort which requires O(n) auxiliary space.
The array is divided by the pivot into two independent sub-problems. Each sub-array is sorted recursively. No merge step is needed because partitioning places elements in the correct relative order.

quick-sort.details.standard.tradeoffs.pr: Fastest general-purpose sorting in practice — excellent cache locality
In-place — only O(log n) extra space for recursion stack
Average O(n log n) with small constant factors
Widely used in standard libraries (C qsort, Java Arrays.sort for primitives)
Easy to parallelize — sub-arrays are independent after partitioning
quick-sort.details.standard.tradeoffs.co: O(n²) worst case on already sorted or nearly sorted input (Lomuto scheme)
Not stable — equal elements may be reordered
Performance highly dependent on pivot selection
Recursion depth can reach O(n) in worst case, risking stack overflow
quick-sort.details.standard.tradeoffs.wh: Best general-purpose sort for in-memory arrays. Prefer over Merge Sort when auxiliary space is limited. Avoid on nearly sorted data without randomized pivot. For stability, use Merge Sort. For small arrays, Insertion Sort is faster.

quick-sort.depRules.name: Time (Average)
quick-sort.depRules.role: O(n log n) — expected with random data

quick-sort.depRules.name: Time (Worst)
quick-sort.depRules.role: O(n²) — already sorted or all equal elements

quick-sort.depRules.name: Stable
quick-sort.depRules.role: No — partitioning may change relative order

quick-sort.depRules.name: Adaptive
quick-sort.depRules.role: No — depends on pivot, not input order

heap-sort.name: Heap Sort
heap-sort.modes.standard.label: Standard
heap-sort.modes.standard.desc: Max-Heap based Heap Sort: builds a max-heap from the array, then repeatedly extracts the maximum element and places it at the end. Uses sift-down to restore the heap property after each extraction. Guarantees O(n log n) in all cases with O(1) extra space.
heap-sort.details.standard.principles: Build a max-heap from the unsorted array using bottom-up heapify
The max-heap property ensures every parent node is greater than or equal to its children
Repeatedly extract the maximum element (root) and place it at the end of the array
After each extraction, restore the heap property with a sift-down operation from the root
Array indices encode the tree implicitly: for node at index i, left child = 2i+1, right child = 2i+2

heap-sort.details.standard.concepts.definition: A complete binary tree stored in an array where every parent ≥ its children (max-heap). The root at index 0 always holds the maximum value.
Starting at a node, compare with both children and swap with the larger child if necessary. Repeat down the tree until the heap property is restored. O(log n) per call.
Process all non-leaf nodes from bottom to top, calling heapify on each. Despite n/2 calls to heapify, the total work is O(n) due to decreasing tree heights at lower levels.
Swap the root (maximum) with the last unsorted element, reduce the heap size by one, and sift-down the new root. Each extraction costs O(log n).

heap-sort.details.standard.tradeoffs.pro: Guaranteed O(n log n) time in all cases — no worst-case degradation
In-place sorting — only O(1) extra memory
No worst-case degradation unlike Quick Sort on sorted input
Combines best of Merge Sort (guaranteed performance) and Quick Sort (in-place)
Often used as the fallback in Introsort to guarantee worst-case bounds
heap-sort.details.standard.tradeoffs.con: Not stable — equal elements may change relative order during heap operations
Poor cache locality due to non-sequential parent-child memory access patterns
Typically slower than Quick Sort in practice due to higher constant factors
More complex to implement correctly than simpler O(n²) algorithms
heap-sort.details.standard.tradeoffs.whe: Use when guaranteed O(n log n) worst-case is required with O(1) space. Ideal for systems with strict memory constraints. Often part of Introsort (hybrid with Quick Sort). Prefer Quick Sort for average-case speed, Merge Sort when stability is needed.

heap-sort.depRules.name: Time (Best)
heap-sort.depRules.role: O(n log n) — always builds heap and extracts

heap-sort.depRules.name: Stable
heap-sort.depRules.role: No — heap operations may reorder equal elements

heap-sort.depRules.name: Adaptive
heap-sort.depRules.role: No — always O(n log n) regardless of input

counting-sort.name: Counting Sort
counting-sort.modes.standard.label: Standard
counting-sort.modes.standard.desc: Counts occurrences of each value, computes a cumulative (prefix) sum, then places each element into its final output position — O(n+k), non-comparison-based and stable.
counting-sort.details.standard.principle: Identify the range of values k in the input (e.g., 1..9)
Phase 1 — Count: iterate input; increment count[value] for each element
Phase 2 — Cumulative Sum: convert count[] to prefix sums so count[v] is the last output position of value v
Phase 3 — Place: scan input right-to-left; for each element v, place it at output[count[v]-1] and decrement count[v]
Right-to-left scan is essential for stability — equal keys keep their original relative order

counting-sort.details.standard.concepts.definition: Auxiliary array of size k+1 that records the frequency of each distinct value. After the counting phase, count[v] = number of occurrences of v.
Transforms count[] so that count[v] equals the number of elements less-than-or-equal-to v. This gives the one-past-last output position for each value.
Scanning input right-to-left and decrementing count[v] after each placement ensures equal keys keep their original order — a prerequisite for using Counting Sort inside Radix Sort.
No element-to-element comparisons are performed — Counting Sort uses value identity as an index, bypassing the Ω(n log n) comparison-sort lower bound.

counting-sort.details.standard.tradeoffs: O(n + k) time — linear when k is bounded
Stable sorting — preserves relative order of equal keys
Simple to implement — three clean phases
Acts as the stable inner sort for Radix Sort
Predictable: performance independent of input order
Only works on integer keys (or keys mappable to integers)
O(k) extra space — impractical if k ≫ n
Not in-place — requires a separate output array
Not useful for general comparable data (strings, custom objects without ordering keys)

Use Counting Sort when keys are small integers within a bounded range (k = O(n)), when stability is required, or as the inner sort in Radix Sort. Avoid when the value range k is much larger than the input size n.

counting-sort.depRules.name: Time (Best/Avg/Worst)
counting-sort.depRules.role: O(n + k) — linear in input size and value range

counting-sort.depRules.name: Space
counting-sort.depRules.role: O(n + k) — count array + output array

counting-sort.depRules.name: Stable
counting-sort.depRules.role: Yes — right-to-left placement preserves relative order

counting-sort.depRules.name: Adaptive
counting-sort.depRules.role: No — always O(n+k) regardless of input order

counting-sort.depRules.name: Input Constraint
counting-sort.depRules.role: Integer keys with bounded range k

counting-sort.depRules.name: In-Place
counting-sort.depRules.role: No — requires auxiliary count and output arrays

radix-sort.name: Radix Sort
radix-sort.modes.lsd.label: LSD
radix-sort.modes.lsd.desc: Least Significant Digit radix sort: performs a stable distribution sort by each digit, from the ones place upward. After d passes (d = number of digits in the maximum value), the array is fully sorted.
radix-sort.details.lsd.principles: Determine d — the number of digits in the largest value (for max 999: d = 3)
For each digit position from least-significant to most-significant: perform a stable distribution sort by that digit
Distribute: scan array left-to-right; place each element into bucket[digit] based on the current digit
Collect: concatenate buckets 0..9 back into the array in order
After all d passes, the array is fully sorted — stability of each pass is what makes this correct

radix-sort.details.lsd.concepts.definition: The rightmost digit is processed first, then tens, then hundreds, etc. This order, combined with a stable inner sort, guarantees a fully sorted array after d passes.
Each digit pass must be stable — elements with the same current digit keep their relative order from the previous pass. Without stability, earlier digit information would be lost.
Radix b (typically 10 for decimal) auxiliary containers. Each pass distributes elements into buckets by the current digit, then collects them back to form the next intermediate array.
For value v and position p (0 = ones): digit = floor(v / 10^p) mod 10. Each pass uses this formula to pick the current digit being sorted.

radix-sort.details.lsd.tradeoffs.pros: O(d · n) time — linear when d is a small constant
Beats O(n log n) comparison sorts for fixed-width integer keys
Stable — preserves relative order of equal keys
Parallelizable — each bucket can be processed independently
Excellent for sorting large datasets of integers or fixed-length strings
radix-sort.details.lsd.tradeoffs.cons: Only works with integer or fixed-width keys — not general comparable data
Requires O(n + b) extra memory for buckets and output
d passes over the data — many cache misses for large n
Harder to implement than quicksort; radix choice affects performance
radix-sort.details.lsd.tradeoffs.whenToU: Use Radix Sort for large arrays of integers with bounded width, for fixed-length strings, or when stability is required and keys fit a digit model. Avoid when keys are truly variable-length.

radix-sort.depRules.name: Time
radix-sort.depRules.role: O(d · (n + b)) — d digit passes over n elements, base b

radix-sort.depRules.name: Stable
radix-sort.depRules.role: Yes — required for correctness of LSD radix sort

radix-sort.depRules.name: Adaptive
radix-sort.depRules.role: No — always d passes regardless of order

radix-sort.depRules.name: In-Place
radix-sort.depRules.role: No — auxiliary buckets and output needed

av.log.js_jump: Jump to arr[{jumpPos}]={value} vs target={target} (block from {blockStart}, step={jumpSize})
linear-search.name: Linear Search
linear-search.modes.standard.label: Standard
linear-search.modes.standard.desc: Classic Linear Search: scans the array from left to right, comparing each element with the target value. Returns the index of the first match or reports that the target is not found.
linear-search.details.standard.principle: Start from the first element and move sequentially to the right
Compare each element with the target value
If a match is found, return its index immediately
If the end of the array is reached without a match, report "not found"
No preprocessing or sorting of the input is required

linear-search.details.standard.concepts.definition: Visiting each element one by one in order. The simplest search strategy with no special data structure requirements.
The fundamental operation: check if arr[i] equals the target. Each comparison eliminates exactly one element.
The value being searched for. Linear Search works with any data type that supports equality comparison.
Place the target at the end of the array as a sentinel to eliminate the bounds check in each iteration, reducing constant factors.

linear-search.details.standard.tradeoffs: Simplest search algorithm — easy to understand and implement
Works on unsorted arrays — no preprocessing needed
O(1) extra space — no auxiliary data structures
Good for small datasets or one-time searches
Works with any data type that supports equality check
O(n) time — slow for large datasets
Does not exploit sorted order — Binary Search is faster for sorted arrays
Must scan the entire array when the target is absent
No benefit from repeated queries on the same data
Best for small or unsorted collections, one-time searches, or when the cost of sorting exceeds the search benefit. For sorted data or frequent queries, prefer Binary Search or hash-based lookups.

linear-search.depRules.name: Time (Best)
linear-search.depRules.role: O(1) — target is the first element

linear-search.depRules.name: Time (Worst)
linear-search.depRules.role: O(n) — target at the end or absent

binary-search.name: Binary Search
binary-search.modes.standard.label: Standard
binary-search.modes.standard.desc: Classic Binary Search: repeatedly divides a sorted array in half, comparing the middle element with the target. Eliminates half the remaining elements with each comparison, achieving O(log n) time.
binary-search.details.standard.principle: The array must be sorted before binary search can be applied
Compare the target with the middle element of the current range
If the target equals the middle element, the search is complete
If the target is smaller, discard the right half and search the left
If the target is larger, discard the left half and search the right

binary-search.details.standard.concepts.definition: A strategy that splits a problem into smaller subproblems. Binary Search halves the search space with each comparison, reducing the problem size exponentially.
The range of indices [low..high] where the target may exist. Initially the entire array; shrinks by half after each comparison.
mid = floor((low + high) / 2). Picks the center of the current range. Using floor avoids overflow that (low + high) / 2 can cause in some languages.
Each comparison eliminates half the remaining elements. For n elements, at most ⌈log₂ n⌉ + 1 comparisons are needed.

binary-search.details.standard.tradeoffs: O(log n) time — extremely fast for large datasets
O(1) extra space — no auxiliary data structures needed
Simple and predictable — easy to analyze and implement
Optimal for sorted static data with frequent lookups
Requires sorted input — sorting costs O(n log n) if not already sorted
Not efficient for small arrays — linear search has lower overhead
Poor for dynamic data — insertions/deletions break sorted order
Only works with random-access data structures (arrays, not linked lists)

Best for large, sorted, static datasets with frequent search queries. If the data changes often, consider balanced BSTs or hash tables. For small arrays (n < 16), linear search may be faster due to lower overhead.

binary-search.depRules.name: Time (Best)
binary-search.depRules.role: O(1) — target is the middle element

binary-search.depRules.name: Time (Worst)
binary-search.depRules.role: O(log n) — target absent or at boundary

jump-search.name: Jump Search
jump-search.modes.standard.label: Standard
jump-search.modes.standard.desc: Classic Jump Search: jumps ahead by √n blocks through a sorted array, then performs a linear scan within the identified block. Achieves O(√n) time — faster than linear, simpler than binary.
jump-search.details.standard.principles: The array must be sorted before applying Jump Search
Jump ahead by a fixed block size (√n) checking the boundary element
If the boundary element is less than the target, skip the entire block
Once a block is found where the target may reside, perform a linear scan within it
The optimal block size √n minimizes the total number of comparisons

jump-search.details.standard.concepts.definition: The optimal jump step is the square root of the array length. This balances the number of jumps (√n) with the linear scan size (√n), yielding O(√n) total comparisons.
The first phase skips entire blocks by checking only the last element. If arr[blockEnd] < target, the target cannot be in this block, so we jump ahead.
Once the correct block is identified, a sequential search within the block finds the exact position. This is at most √n comparisons.
The step size √n minimizes the worst case: too small means too many scans, too large means too many jumps. √n is the mathematical sweet spot.

jump-search.details.standard.tradeoffs.p: O(√n) time — significantly faster than linear search
Simpler to implement than binary search
Only requires backward traversal once — good for systems where jumping back is costly
O(1) extra space — no recursion or auxiliary structures
Works well when binary search overhead is not justified
jump-search.details.standard.tradeoffs.c: Slower than binary search O(log n) for large datasets
Requires sorted input — sorting costs O(n log n)
Fixed block size may be suboptimal for non-uniform data distributions
Not adaptive — does not narrow search space as aggressively as binary search
jump-search.details.standard.tradeoffs.w: Best when the data is sorted and backward traversal is expensive (e.g., linked lists with forward-only iteration). Also useful when simplicity is preferred over optimal performance. For arrays, binary search is usually better.

jump-search.depRules.name: Time (Best)
jump-search.depRules.role: O(1) — target at first jump boundary

jump-search.depRules.name: Time (Worst)
jump-search.depRules.role: O(√n) — target near end of block

interpolation-search.name: Interpolation Search
interpolation-search.modes.standard.desc: Interpolation Search estimates the probe position using the interpolation formula, adapting to the data distribution. Achieves O(log log n) on uniform data but degrades to O(n) on skewed distributions.
interpolation-search.details.standard.pr: The array must be sorted before interpolation search can be applied
Estimate probe position using: pos = low + ⌊((target − arr[low]) / (arr[high] − arr[low])) × (high − low)⌋
If the probe element equals the target, the search is complete
If the probe element is less than the target, eliminate the left portion and search right
If the probe element is greater, eliminate the right portion and search left

interpolation-search.details.standard.co.definition: pos = low + ⌊((target − arr[low]) / (arr[high] − arr[low])) × (high − low)⌋. Estimates where the target would fall if values are uniformly distributed between arr[low] and arr[high].
Unlike binary search’s fixed midpoint, the probe adapts to the data. For uniform data it jumps closer to the target, reducing comparisons dramatically.
Values spaced roughly evenly. Interpolation search excels here because the formula accurately predicts the target’s location, achieving O(log log n) comparisons.
If values are clustered or exponentially distributed, the probe estimate is poor, causing O(n) behavior similar to linear search. Binary search is safer for unknown distributions.

interpolation-search.details.standard.tr: O(log log n) average on uniformly distributed data — faster than binary search
O(1) extra space — no auxiliary data structures needed
Adaptive — adjusts probe position based on value distribution
Excellent for large datasets with known uniform distribution
Same worst-case as linear search but vastly better on typical data
O(n) worst case on skewed or adversarial data
Requires sorted input — sorting costs O(n log n) if not already sorted
Division by zero risk when arr[low] equals arr[high]
More complex formula than binary search’s simple midpoint
Performance depends heavily on data distribution — not always predictable

Best for large, sorted datasets with roughly uniform value distribution (e.g., database keys, timestamps, sequential IDs). For non-uniform data or small arrays, binary search is safer and more predictable.

interpolation-search.depRules.name: Time (Average)
interpolation-search.depRules.role: O(log log n) — on uniform data

av.queue.label: Queue:
bfs.name: BFS
bfs.modes.standard.label: Standard
bfs.modes.standard.desc: Classic BFS: explores a graph level by level using a FIFO queue. Starting from node A, it visits all neighbors before moving to the next depth level, producing a wave-like traversal pattern.
bfs.details.standard.principles: Start from a source node and mark it as visited
Add the source to a FIFO queue
While the queue is not empty: dequeue a node, explore all unvisited neighbors
Each neighbor is enqueued and marked as visited

bfs.details.standard.concepts.definition: First-In-First-Out data structure. Nodes discovered first are explored first, ensuring breadth-first order.
All nodes at distance k from the source are explored before any node at distance k+1. This is the key property of BFS.
Tracks which nodes have been seen to prevent revisiting. Essential for correctness in graphs with cycles.
In unweighted graphs, BFS naturally finds the shortest path (fewest edges) from the source to every reachable node.

bfs.details.standard.tradeoffs.pros: Finds the shortest path in unweighted graphs
Complete — guaranteed to find a solution if reachable
Optimal for finding nearest neighbors or closest nodes
Foundation for many advanced algorithms (Dijkstra, A*)
bfs.details.standard.tradeoffs.cons: O(V) space for the queue — can be large for wide graphs
Not suitable for weighted graphs — use Dijkstra instead
Explores all nodes at current depth before going deeper
Less memory-efficient than DFS for deep, narrow graphs
bfs.details.standard.tradeoffs.whenToUse: Use BFS when you need the shortest path in unweighted graphs, want to explore nodes by distance from source, or need level-order traversal. For weighted graphs, use Dijkstra. For exhaustive deep exploration, consider DFS.

bfs.depRules.name: Time
bfs.depRules.role: O(V + E) — visits every vertex and edge once

bfs.depRules.name: Shortest Path
bfs.depRules.role: Yes — finds shortest path in unweighted graphs

bfs.depRules.name: Complete
bfs.depRules.role: Yes — always finds a solution if one exists

bfs.depRules.name: Weighted Graphs
bfs.depRules.role: No — does not handle edge weights (use Dijkstra)

av.stack.label: Stack:
dfs.name: DFS
dfs.modes.standard.label: Standard
dfs.modes.standard.desc: Classic DFS: explores a graph as deep as possible before backtracking, using a LIFO stack. Starting from node A, it dives down one path until reaching a dead-end, then backtracks to explore unvisited branches.
dfs.details.standard.principles: Start from a source node and push it onto a LIFO stack
While the stack is not empty: pop a node from the top
If the node has not been visited, mark it as visited and explore its neighbors
Push all unvisited neighbors onto the stack
This naturally explores as deeply as possible before backtracking

dfs.details.standard.concepts.definition: Last-In-First-Out data structure. The most recently discovered node is explored first, producing a depth-first traversal.
When DFS reaches a dead-end (no unvisited neighbors), it returns to the most recent node with unexplored branches.
Tracks which nodes have been seen to prevent revisiting. Essential for correctness in graphs with cycles.
DFS explores paths to maximum depth before branching. It does not guarantee visiting nodes in order of distance from source.

dfs.details.standard.tradeoffs.pros: Memory-efficient for deep, narrow graphs — stack depth is O(V) worst case
Natural basis for topological sorting of DAGs
Detects cycles efficiently via back-edge detection
Foundation for finding connected components and strongly connected components
dfs.details.standard.tradeoffs.cons: Does not find shortest paths — path found depends on neighbor ordering
Can get stuck in deep branches if graph is very deep
Traversal order depends on neighbor ordering — not deterministic without sorting
Less intuitive than BFS for distance-related problems
dfs.details.standard.tradeoffs.whenToUse: Use DFS when you need to explore all reachable nodes, detect cycles, perform topological sorting, or find connected components. For shortest paths in unweighted graphs, use BFS instead.

dfs.depRules.name: Time
dfs.depRules.role: O(V + E) — visits every vertex and edge once

dfs.depRules.name: Shortest Path
dfs.depRules.role: No — does not guarantee shortest path

dfs.depRules.name: Complete
dfs.depRules.role: Yes — always finds a solution if one exists

dfs.depRules.name: Cycle Detection
dfs.depRules.role: Yes — naturally detects back edges / cycles

bst-operations.name: BST Operations
bst-operations.modes.insert.label: Insert
bst-operations.modes.insert.desc: BST Insert: builds a Binary Search Tree by inserting values one at a time. Each value is compared with nodes starting from the root, going left if smaller or right if larger, until an empty position is found.
bst-operations.details.insert.principles: Start at the root of the tree
Compare the new value with the current node
If smaller, go to the left child; if larger or equal, go to the right child
Repeat until an empty position (null child) is found
Place the new value at that empty position

bst-operations.details.insert.concepts.definition: For every node, all values in the left subtree are smaller, and all values in the right subtree are larger. This invariant is maintained after every insertion.
The number of edges on the longest root-to-leaf path. Determines the worst-case time for insert and search operations.
A balanced BST has O(log n) height. A degenerate BST (all nodes in one direction) has O(n) height, reducing to a linked list.
Visiting left subtree, then root, then right subtree produces values in sorted order. This is a key property of BSTs.

Simple insertion — just follow comparisons down the tree
O(log n) average case for insert, search, and delete
In-order traversal gives sorted output without extra work
Foundation for more advanced trees (AVL, Red-Black)
Dynamic — handles insertions and deletions efficiently
O(n) worst case if input is sorted (degenerate tree)
Not self-balancing — can become highly unbalanced
No guaranteed O(log n) — depends on insertion order
Extra memory for node pointers compared to arrays

Use basic BST for educational purposes or when input is known to be random. For guaranteed O(log n) operations, use self-balancing variants: AVL Tree for read-heavy workloads, Red-Black Tree for write-heavy workloads.

bst-operations.depRules.name: Sorted Output
bst-operations.depRules.role: Yes — in-order traversal gives sorted sequence

bst-operations.depRules.name: Self-Balancing
bst-operations.depRules.role: No — use AVL or Red-Black Tree

av.log.dp_base: Base case: F({index}) = {value} — defined, no computation needed
av.log.dp_read: To compute F({computing}): read F({i1})={v1}, F({i2})={v2}
av.log.dp_result: Answer: F({n}) = {value} — all subproblems solved bottom-up
fibonacci.name: Fibonacci
fibonacci.modes.bottom-up.label: Bottom-Up
fibonacci.modes.bottom-up.desc: Iterative tabulation: builds the DP table from F(0) to F(n), filling each cell using previously computed values. O(n) time and space.
fibonacci.modes.top-down.label: Top-Down
fibonacci.modes.top-down.desc: Recursive with memoization: computes F(n) by recursing into subproblems, caching results to avoid recomputation. O(n) time.
fibonacci.modes.naive.label: Naive Recursive
fibonacci.modes.naive.desc: Pure recursion without memoization. The full recursion tree reveals O(2^n) exponential time from redundant recomputation.
fibonacci.details.bottom-up.principles: Fill DP table iteratively from index 2 to n
Read previously computed values — no recomputation

fibonacci.details.bottom-up.concepts.definition: Bottom-up DP approach: solve all subproblems in order, starting from the smallest, and store results in a table.
F(n) depends on F(n-1) and F(n-2). Each Fibonacci number is built from two previously solved subproblems.
Without DP, F(n-2) would be computed twice: once for F(n) and once for F(n-1). Tabulation eliminates this redundancy.
Since F(i) only depends on the two previous values, the table can be reduced to two variables for O(1) space.

fibonacci.details.bottom-up.tradeoffs.pr: O(n) time — each subproblem solved exactly once
Can be further optimized to O(1) space
fibonacci.details.bottom-up.tradeoffs.co: Computes all subproblems even if not all are needed
O(n) space for the full table
Less intuitive than the recursive definition
Not easily generalizable to all DP problems
fibonacci.details.bottom-up.tradeoffs.wh: Use bottom-up when you need all subproblem values, want predictable performance, or need to avoid stack overflow for large n. Preferred for production code.
fibonacci.details.top-down.principles: Start from F(n) and recurse into F(n-1) and F(n-2)
Before computing, check if result is already in memo cache
If cached (memo hit), return immediately — no recomputation
If not cached, compute recursively and store the result

fibonacci.details.top-down.concepts.definition: Top-down DP technique: cache results of expensive function calls and return the cached result when the same inputs occur again.
When a recursive call finds a previously computed result in the cache. This eliminates the entire subtree of redundant computation.
Each recursive call adds a frame to the stack. Maximum depth is n, giving O(n) space for the call stack alone.
Unlike bottom-up, memoization only computes subproblems that are actually needed. For Fibonacci this makes no difference, but for sparse DP problems it can be faster.

fibonacci.details.top-down.tradeoffs.pro: Natural recursive structure mirrors the mathematical definition
Easy to derive from naive recursive solution
Good for problems with large sparse state spaces
fibonacci.details.top-down.tradeoffs.con: Recursion overhead — function call cost per subproblem
O(n) call stack depth — risk of stack overflow for large n
Harder to optimize space compared to bottom-up
fibonacci.details.top-down.tradeoffs.whe: Use top-down when the recursive structure is natural, when only a subset of subproblems is needed, or as a first step in developing a DP solution before converting to bottom-up.
fibonacci.details.naive.principles: Directly implement the recurrence: F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2)
Each call spawns two recursive calls
No caching — identical subproblems are recomputed many times

fibonacci.details.naive.concepts.definition: Without memoization, the number of calls grows as O(2^n). F(6) alone requires 25 function calls, most of which are redundant.
F(3) is computed 3 times for F(6), F(2) is computed 5 times. Each duplicate call spawns its own subtree of duplicates.
A tree where each node represents a function call. The left child is F(n-1), the right is F(n-2). Duplicate nodes show the waste.
The naive approach demonstrates exactly why dynamic programming exists: to eliminate redundant computation through caching (memoization) or tabulation.

fibonacci.details.naive.tradeoffs.pros: Simplest implementation — directly matches math definition
Good for understanding the problem before optimization
fibonacci.details.naive.tradeoffs.cons: O(2^n) time — completely impractical for n > 30
Demonstrates the anti-pattern that DP solves
fibonacci.details.naive.tradeoffs.whenTo: Use only for educational purposes to demonstrate why dynamic programming is needed. Never use in production.
av.log.str_mismatch_start: Mismatch at text[{ti}]: “{tc}”≠“{pc}” — at pattern[0], advance text
av.log.str_lps_set_zero: LPS[{index}]=0: no proper prefix equals suffix
av.log.str_lps_set_base: LPS[0]=0: single char — no proper prefix exists
kmp.name: KMP
kmp.modes.standard.label: KMP
kmp.modes.standard.desc: KMP algorithm: builds the LPS (Longest Proper Prefix which is also Suffix) array for the pattern, then scans the text using the LPS array to skip redundant comparisons on mismatch.
kmp.details.standard.principles: Build the LPS array for the pattern in O(m) time
Scan the text left to right; the text pointer never moves backward
On match: advance both text and pattern pointers
On mismatch: use LPS[j−1] to shift the pattern, avoiding redundant comparisons
When pattern is fully matched (j == m), record the match and continue using LPS

kmp.details.standard.concepts.definition: Knuth–Morris–Pratt (1977) — a linear-time string matching algorithm. Instead of re-scanning characters after a mismatch, it uses a precomputed LPS array to shift the pattern intelligently, achieving O(n+m) time where n is text length and m is pattern length.

Longest Proper Prefix which is also Suffix. For each position i in the pattern, LPS[i] stores the length of the longest proper prefix of pattern[0..i] that is also a suffix. This is the key data structure that tells the algorithm how far to shift on mismatch.
A prefix that is not the entire string. For "ABAB", proper prefixes are "", "A", "AB", "ABA" — not "ABAB" itself.
On mismatch at pattern[j], the LPS tells us that pattern[0..LPS[j−1]−1] already matches the text. We set j = LPS[j−1] instead of restarting from 0.
Unlike naive string matching, the text index i never decreases. This guarantees O(n) scanning time regardless of the pattern.

kmp.details.standard.tradeoffs.pros: O(n + m) worst-case time — optimal for single-pattern matching
No backtracking on the text pointer — suitable for streaming data
Finds all occurrences in a single pass
Deterministic — no worst-case degradation (unlike naive O(nm))
kmp.details.standard.tradeoffs.cons: O(m) extra space for the LPS array
More complex to implement than naive string matching
For very short patterns, naive approach may be faster due to lower overhead
Single-pattern only — for multiple patterns use Aho-Corasick
kmp.details.standard.tradeoffs.whenToUse: Use KMP when you need guaranteed linear-time string matching, especially for long texts or patterns with repeated prefixes. Ideal for streaming data where backtracking is impossible.

kmp.depRules.name: Time (Searching)
kmp.depRules.role: O(n) — single scan with LPS-guided shifts

kmp.depRules.name: Backtracking
kmp.depRules.role: No — text pointer never moves backward

av.log.hash_not_found: Value "{key}" not found in hash table
hash-table.name: Hash Table
hash-table.modes.chaining.label: Chaining
hash-table.modes.chaining.desc: Separate Chaining: each bucket holds a linked list of values. On collision, the new key is appended to the chain.
hash-table.modes.linear-probing.label: Linear Probing
hash-table.modes.linear-probing.desc: Open Addressing with Linear Probing: each bucket holds at most one value. On collision, probe sequentially to find an empty slot.
hash-table.modes.search.label: Search
hash-table.modes.search.desc: Search for a target value in a pre-populated hash table. Computes the hash, then traverses the chain until found or exhausted.
hash-table.details.chaining.principles: Compute the hash index: h(key) = charSum(key) mod tableSize
If the bucket at index h is empty, insert the key directly
If the bucket is occupied (collision), append the key to the linked list (chain)
Each bucket can hold an unlimited number of keys via chaining
Load factor α = n/m can exceed 1 without table restructuring

hash-table.details.chaining.concepts.definition: Maps a key to a bucket index. h(k) = charSum(k) mod m sums the character codes of the key.
Two different keys hash to the same bucket index. Inevitable by the Pigeonhole Principle.
Each bucket stores a linked list of all keys that hash to that index.
Ratio α = n/m. Average chain length equals α.
Assumption that each key is equally likely to hash to any bucket, giving O(1) average.

hash-table.details.chaining.tradeoffs.pr: Simple to implement — just a linked list per bucket
Handles any load factor without resizing
hash-table.details.chaining.tradeoffs.co: Extra memory for linked list pointers
O(n) worst case when all keys collide
hash-table.details.chaining.tradeoffs.wh: Use separate chaining when the load factor is unpredictable, deletions are frequent, or simplicity is preferred.
hash-table.details.linear-probing.princi: Compute the hash index: h(key) = charSum(key) mod tableSize
If the slot is empty, insert the key directly
If occupied, probe to the next slot: (h + 1) mod m, (h + 2) mod m, ...
Continue probing until an empty slot is found

hash-table.details.linear-probing.concep.definition: All keys stored directly in the table. On collision, probe alternative slots.
On collision at h, try h+1, h+2, h+3, etc. (mod table size).
Consecutive occupied slots form clusters, degrading performance.
At α=0.5, avg probes ≈ 1.5; at α=0.9, avg probes ≈ 5.5.
Deleted slots marked as 'deleted' to avoid breaking probe chains.

hash-table.details.linear-probing.tradeo: Good performance at low load factors
Use linear probing when memory is limited, cache performance matters, and load factor stays low (< 0.7).
hash-table.details.search.principles: Compute the hash: h(target) = charSum(target) mod tableSize
Go to the bucket at index h
Traverse the chain comparing each node with the target

hash-table.details.search.concepts.definition: Target exists. Average probes: 1 + α/2 for chaining.
Target absent. Average probes: 1 + α for chaining.
Number of slots examined. Depends on load factor and collision strategy.

hash-table.details.search.tradeoffs.pros: Consistent performance regardless of insertion order
hash-table.details.search.tradeoffs.cons: O(n) worst case with many collisions
No range queries or ordered traversal
hash-table.details.search.tradeoffs.when: Use hash-based search for exact-match lookups where average O(1) time is critical.

hash-table.depRules.name: Time (Average Insert)
hash-table.depRules.role: O(1) — constant with good hash function

hash-table.depRules.name: Time (Average Search)
hash-table.depRules.role: O(1) — constant with good hash function

hash-table.depRules.name: Time (Worst Insert)
hash-table.depRules.role: O(n) — all keys collide to same bucket

hash-table.depRules.name: Time (Worst Search)
hash-table.depRules.role: O(n) — degenerate chain or full table

hash-table.depRules.name: Space
hash-table.depRules.role: O(n + m) — n keys + m buckets

hash-table.depRules.name: Hash Function
hash-table.depRules.role: h(k) = charSum(k) mod m — sum of character codes

av.pq.label: Priority Queue:
av.log.complete_dijkstra: Dijkstra complete: shortest paths found to all {totalVisited}/{totalNodes} nodes
dijkstra.name: Dijkstra
dijkstra.modes.standard.label: Standard
dijkstra.modes.standard.desc: Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest path from a source node to all other nodes in a weighted graph. It uses a priority queue to greedily extract the nearest unvisited node and relaxes its neighbors' distances.
dijkstra.details.standard.principles: Initialize source distance to 0 and all other distances to ∞
Add source node to the priority queue with distance 0
Extract the node with minimum distance from the priority queue
For each neighbor: if current dist + edge weight < known dist, update (relax)
Mark the extracted node as finalized and repeat until queue is empty

dijkstra.details.standard.concepts.definition: Always returns the element with the smallest key. Ensures we process the closest unfinalized node first.
Updating a neighbor's distance when a shorter path is found: if dist[u] + weight(u,v) < dist[v], update dist[v].
Always processes the nearest unvisited node. With non-negative weights, this guarantees optimality.
The set of edges forming the shortest paths from source to all reachable nodes.

dijkstra.details.standard.tradeoffs.pros: Finds optimal shortest paths in weighted graphs with non-negative weights
More efficient than Bellman-Ford for sparse graphs: O((V+E)log V) vs O(VE)
Greedy approach guarantees correctness without revisiting finalized nodes
Foundation for A*, bidirectional Dijkstra, and other pathfinding variants
Natural fit for GPS routing, network routing, and map navigation
dijkstra.details.standard.tradeoffs.cons: Cannot handle negative edge weights — use Bellman-Ford instead
Priority queue adds complexity compared to simple BFS
O((V+E)log V) is slower than BFS O(V+E) for unweighted graphs
Only computes single-source shortest paths — use Floyd-Warshall for all-pairs
dijkstra.details.standard.tradeoffs.when: Use Dijkstra when you need shortest paths in weighted graphs with non-negative weights. For unweighted graphs, BFS is simpler. For negative weights, use Bellman-Ford.

dijkstra.depRules.name: Time
dijkstra.depRules.role: O((V + E) log V) — with min-heap priority queue

dijkstra.depRules.name: Shortest Path
dijkstra.depRules.role: Yes — finds optimal shortest paths from source

dijkstra.depRules.name: Complete
dijkstra.depRules.role: Yes — finds paths to all reachable nodes

av.log.complete_a-star_no_path: A* complete: no path found ({totalExpanded}/{totalNodes} expanded)
a-star.name: A*
a-star.modes.standard.label: Standard
a-star.modes.standard.desc: A* finds the lowest-cost path from a start node to a goal node by ordering the frontier by f(n) = g(n) + h(n): the known cost from start plus a heuristic estimate to the goal. With an admissible heuristic, A* is optimal and typically expands fewer nodes than Dijkstra.
a-star.details.standard.principles: Initialize g(start)=0, g(n)=∞ otherwise; precompute h(n) for every node
Push start into the open set with f(start) = g(start) + h(start)
Extract the node with minimum f from the open set
If it is the goal, reconstruct the path by walking parent pointers back to start
Otherwise for each neighbor: tentative_g = g(u) + weight(u,v); if better than g(v), relax and push with new f
Move the extracted node into the closed set; repeat until goal is reached or open set is empty

a-star.details.standard.concepts.definition: The best known actual cost from the start node to node n. Updated whenever a cheaper path is discovered (relaxation).
An estimate of the remaining cost from n to the goal. Must be admissible (never overestimate) to preserve optimality.
f(n) = g(n) + h(n). The priority key used to order the open set: smaller f means more promising node.
Priority queue of discovered nodes that have not yet been expanded. Ordered by f-score.
Nodes already expanded with a finalized g-score. With a consistent heuristic, they do not need re-opening.
h(n) ≤ true cost from n to the goal for every n. Guarantees A* finds an optimal path.
For every edge (u,v): h(u) ≤ weight(u,v) + h(v). Implies admissibility and allows skipping re-opens.

a-star.details.standard.tradeoffs.pros: Optimal path guaranteed when the heuristic is admissible
Typically expands far fewer nodes than Dijkstra for single-target queries
Tunable: heuristic quality directly controls efficiency vs optimality
Basis for GPS routing, game AI pathfinding, robotics, puzzle solving
Generalizes Dijkstra (h≡0) and Greedy Best-First (g≡0) in one framework
a-star.details.standard.tradeoffs.cons: Requires a domain-specific heuristic — not always obvious how to design
Memory-heavy: stores both open and closed sets plus parent pointers
A non-admissible heuristic breaks optimality (may find suboptimal paths)
With a poor heuristic, performance collapses toward Dijkstra
a-star.details.standard.tradeoffs.whenTo: Use A* for single-source-single-target shortest paths when a good admissible heuristic is available. For all-pairs or no heuristic, use Dijkstra. For negative weights, use Bellman-Ford.

a-star.depRules.name: Time
a-star.depRules.role: O((V + E) log V) — with min-heap priority queue

a-star.depRules.name: Space
a-star.depRules.role: O(V) — open set + closed set + parents

a-star.depRules.name: Optimality
a-star.depRules.role: Yes — when the heuristic h(n) is admissible

a-star.depRules.name: Completeness
a-star.depRules.role: Yes — in finite graphs with non-negative weights

a-star.depRules.name: Admissibility
a-star.depRules.role: Required — h(n) must never overestimate true cost

a-star.depRules.name: Heuristic Quality
a-star.depRules.role: Better h → fewer expansions; h≡0 degrades to Dijkstra

a-star.depRules.name: Data Structure
a-star.depRules.role: Priority Queue keyed by f = g + h
